---
title: "3.3 Does Scaling-in Work?"
subtitle: "Chapter 03. Implementing Mean Reversion Strategies"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: false
include-after-body:
  - ../../../../styles/web-note-modal.html
---

# Scaling-in Is Intuitive but Not Automatically Optimal

Scaling-in, or averaging-in, is one of the most natural ideas in mean-reversion
trading. If a spread deviates farther from the mean, the eventual reversion
profit looks larger, so adding capital seems sensible.

Chan points out that the intuition is incomplete. The relevant question is not
whether larger deviations create larger potential payoff. They do. The question
is whether splitting the entry into stages is better than going all-in early or
waiting and going all-in later.

# Chan's Two-Path Comparison

Suppose the price has fallen to $L_1$ and is expected eventually to reach a
higher value $F > L_1$. There is probability $p$ that price first falls further
to $L_2 < L_1$ before reverting, and probability $1-p$ that it reverts
immediately from $L_1$ to $F$.

Assume enough buying power for exactly two contracts. Then compare:

1. all-in at $L_1$
2. all-in at $L_2$
3. average-in: one contract at $L_1$, one at $L_2$

The expected profits are:

$$
\text{I. } 2(F-L_1)
$$

$$
\text{II. } 2p(F-L_2)
$$

$$
\text{III. } p[(F-L_1)+(F-L_2)] + (1-p)(F-L_1)
= (F-L_1) + p(F-L_2).
$$

# Why Average-in Never Wins in This Static Model

If $p=0$, method I is clearly best because the lower level $L_2$ never arrives.
If $p=1$, method II is best because waiting guarantees the better entry.

More generally, there is a transition probability

$$
\hat{p} = \frac{F-L_1}{F-L_2}
$$

such that:

- if $p < \hat{p}$, all-in at $L_1$ is better than waiting
- if $p > \hat{p}$, all-in at $L_2$ is better than entering early

Chan's important conclusion is that average-in never becomes the best of the
three. It is always dominated by one of the all-in choices in this stylized
in-sample setup.

# Why This Does Not Kill Scaling-in in Live Trading

The argument above assumes the probability structure is fixed. Real markets are
not like that. Volatility changes, the odds of further deviation change, and the
trader often cares about realized Sharpe ratio and out-of-sample stability, not
just in-sample average profit under a frozen model.

That is why Chan still defends scaling-in as a practical technique. Even if it
is never in-sample optimal under the static arithmetic model, it may still
produce better realized risk-adjusted performance out of sample when the market
environment changes.

# Review Checklist

- Is the case for scaling-in based on fixed arithmetic or on changing market
  conditions?
- Would one all-in threshold outperform the multistage rule in sample?
- Is the goal maximum expected profit, or smoother realized Sharpe?
- Does scaling-in reduce market impact enough to matter in live execution?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [3.2 Bollinger Bands]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/chapter-03/sections/02-bollinger-bands/)
- Next: [3.4 Kalman Filter as Dynamic Linear Regression]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/chapter-03/sections/04-kalman-filter-as-dynamic-linear-regression/)
